text: If a plaintext comes from a natural language, such as English, which of the following encryption algorithms can be expected to have a more even (“flatter”) frequency distribution of ciphertext characters?

options.text: The Caesar cipher
The random simple substitution cipher
A transposition cipher on blocks of size 10
The Vigenére cipher with a key of length 5

text: Which of the following is a fundamental weakness of the Hill cipher for any size of encryption matrix?

options.text: The number of possible keys is too small
Encryption is a linear function
The encryption function is computationally expensive
Decryption is not always possible

text: Double DES is the encryption algorithm defined by iterating two instances of the DES algorithm - the initial DES ciphertext is fed back into the encryption algorithm with an independent key. The main disadvantage of double DES is:

options.text: it is vulnerable to differential cryptanalysis;
it has poor avalanche effects;
the key length is too short to resist practical brute-force search;
there is a meet-in-the-middle attack which reduces the effective key length.

text: In an iterative block cipher the purpose of the key schedule is to:

options.text: define how to derive the round keys from the master key;
generate different keys for every block encrypted;
choose between different master keys;
define how the master key is generated.

text: For any given values x and m, the square-and-multiply algorithm when used to compute x66 mod m requires:

options.text: 5 squarings and 3 multiplication modulo m
6 squarings and 1 multiplication modulo m
8 squarings and 1 multiplication modulo m
63 squarings and 1 multiplication modulo m

text: Which of the following pairs of equations cannot be solved using the Chinese Remainder Theorem?

options.text: x ≡3 mod 5 and x ≡3 mod 11
x ≡3 mod 6 and x ≡4 mod 11
x ≡3 mod 5 and x ≡3 mod 12
x ≡3 mod 6 and x ≡4 mod 12

text: Let g be a generator for the integers modulo p. The discrete logarithm problem is:

options.text: given y, find x with y = xg mod p;
given x, find y with y = xg mod p.
given y, find x with y = gx mod p;
given x, find y with y = gx mod p.

text: Which of the following block cipher modes of operation is not designed to provide data confidentiality?

options.text: Counter mode (CTR)
Cipher block chaining (CBC)
Cipher-based MAC (CMAC)
Counter with CBC-MAC (CCM)

text: The main disadvantage of basic Electronic Code Book (ECB) mode of operation for block ciphers, in comparison with counter mode (CTR) and cipher block chaining (CBC) mode, is:

options.text: ECB mode encryption is less efficient;
ECB mode has large error propagation;
equal plaintext blocks in ECB mode give equal ciphertext blocks;
ECB mode requires longer keys.

text: Which of these statements about the keystream used in the one time pad is false?

options.text: The keystream is completely random
The keystream is as long as the message
The keystream is generated by a linear feedback shift register (LFSR)
The keystream is only used once

text: The maximum period of a linear feedback shift register with 10 binary storage locations is:
In the RSA encryption algorithm it is common to use the Chinese Remainder Theorem to:

options.text: speed up the encryption process;
speed up the decryption process;
speed up the key generation process;
all of the above.

text: The RSA encryption scheme uses a public exponent e, a private exponent d, and a public modulus n which is the product of two primes p and q. Regarding security of the scheme it is known that:

options.text: knowledge of n and e is sufficient to find d;
an attacker who can encrypt a random message can find d;
finding d from p and q is as hard as factorising n;
an attacker who can find d is able to also find p and q

text: ElGamal encryption in Z∗ p uses a modulus p, while RSA encryption uses a composite modulus n. When these are chosen to be of the same length:

options.text: RSA ciphertexts and Elgamal ciphertexts are the same size;
RSA ciphertexts and Elgamal ciphertexts are of a random size;
RSA ciphertexts are twice the size of Elgamal ciphertexts;
ElGamal ciphertexts are twice the size of RSA ciphertexts.

text: Three important computational problems in cryptography are: the discrete logarithm problem in Z∗ p (DLP), the discrete logarithm problem in elliptic curves (ECDLP) and the integer factorisation (IF) problem. If full-scale quantum computers become available then we know that:

options.text: all three of these problems will have efficient solutions;
only IF will have an efficient solution;
only DLP will have an efficient solution;
only IF and DLP will have efficient solutions.

text: HMAC is an algorithm often used in TLS. It is based on any iterated hash function. Which of these statements with regard to HMAC is false?

options.text: HMAC takes a shared secret key as one input;
the output size of HMAC is the same as the output size of the hash function;
the message input to HMAC is of variable length;
both the hash function and HMAC provide message integrity.

text: Galois counter mode (GCM) is often used in TLS to provide:

options.text: data confidentiality;
data integrity;
error checking;
authenticated encryption.

text: When public key cryptography is used to provide digital signatures:

options.text: the public key of the signer is required in order to generate the signature;
the public key of the verifier is required in order to generate the signature;
the private key of the signer is required in order to generate the signature;
the private key of the verifier is required in order to generate the signature.

text: The Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA) is a standardised algorithm based on ElGamal signatures. Compared with RSA signatures at the same security level which of the following is true?

options.text: DSA signatures are shorter than RSA signatures;
DSA signatures are more efficient to verify, even if the public RSA exponent equals 3;
DSA signatures cannot use elliptic curve groups but RSA signatures can;
DSA signatures do not require a random input but RSA signatures do.

text: If we choose elements randomly from a set of 1 000 000 values then, according to the birthday paradox, a collision will occur with probability 0.5 after approximately:

options.text: 999 999 elements have been chosen;
500 000 elements have been chosen;
1 000 elements have been chosen;
20 elements have been chosen.

text: When assessing the security of a key establishment protocol such as the Needham- Schroeder protocol, we assume that an attacker is able to:

options.text: obtain any session keys used in previous runs of the protocol;
obtain the long-term key of the parties involved in the protocol run under attack;
break any encryption algorithm used in the protocol;
force any protocol participant to repeat nonce values.

text: Forward secrecy is provided in TLS as long as the handshake protocol uses:

options.text: ephemeral Diffie-Hellman;
static Diffie-Hellman;
RSA with a 1024 bit modulus;
RSA with a 2048 bit modulus.

text: TLS consists of a number of protocols. The protocol responsible for providing confiden- tiality and data integrity to payload data is called:

options.text: the handshake protocol;
the record protocol;
the alert protocol;
the change cipher spec protocol.

text: A difference between the public key infrastructure used by TLS for web browsers, and that provided by PGP for email security, is:

options.text: PGP keys can be signed by any other user;
PGP keys are certified in a hierarchical manner;
PGP keys have no expiry date;
PGP keys can use any type of public key algorithm.

text: Like TLS, IPSec can be used to set up secure communication between nodes. Which of the following applies to IPSec, but not to TLS?

options.text: Different suites of cryptographic algorithms can be used.
Traffic flow confidentiality may be provided.
Forward secrecy may be provided using Diffie-Hellman key exchange.
The protocol specification defines both key establishment and security of user data.

text: A →S : IDA, IDB, NA

parts.solution: There are 264 keys in total so, using the approximation that there are 225 seconds in a year, the attacker must search through 264/225 = 239 keys per second.

text: S →A : {KAB, IDA, IDB, NA}KAS

parts.solution: From the encryption equation, C2 = E(P2 ü C1, K) we see that changing P2 will result in a random change to C2 as long as the encryption algorithm is a strong block cipher. Moreover this will then propagate to C3 = E(P3 ü C2, K) which will also be randomly changed. Similar for C4. Thus the new ciphertext can be expected to be competely different for CÕ 2, CÕ 3, CÕ 4 while CÕ 1 = C1 since its inputs are unchanged.

text: S →B : {KAB, IDA, IDB, NA}KBS
B →A : {IDB, IDA, NA, NB}KAB

parts.solution: B æ A(C) : {IDB, IDA, NA, NB}KÕ AB

text: A →B : {IDA, IDB, NA, NB}KAB Describe a replay attack on this protocol, showing concrete messages sent by the attacker.

parts.text: A →B : {IDA, IDB, NA, NB}KAB Describe a replay attack on this protocol, showing concrete messages sent by the attacker.
parts.solution: A(C) æ B : {IDA, IDB, NA, NB}KÕ AB C takes the roles of both A and S and can compute the correct message 5 since it has the session key and can therefore decrypt message 4 to obtain NB. The exchange looks normal to B who accepts the old key KÕ .

text: Consider the following protocol with the goal of key establishment. Here NA is a nonce chosen by A, NB is a nonce chosen by B, and KAB is the session key chosen by server S. IDA and IDB are identity strings for A and B respectively. KAS and KBS are key- encrypting keys initially shared between S and A, and between S and B respectively. The notation {X}K denotes authenticated encryption of X with key K.

parts.text: Consider the following protocol with the goal of key establishment. Here NA is a nonce chosen by A, NB is a nonce chosen by B, and KAB is the session key chosen by server S. IDA and IDB are identity strings for A and B respectively. KAS and KBS are key- encrypting keys initially shared between S and A, and between S and B respectively. The notation {X}K denotes authenticated encryption of X with key K.
parts.solution: Since B does not receive any freshness guarantee from S the replay must be against him. The attacker can eavesdrop an old token T Õ = {KÕ AB, A, B, NA}KBS, the value NA, and obtain the old key KÕ AB. Then the attacker C masquerades as both S and A and starts a new session with the following messages.

text: Recently it has been widely suggested that secure communications on the Internet should provide forward secrecy.

parts.text: Describe what attack forward secrecy prevents when provided on a TLS connection.
parts.solution: The attack would be that the adversary obtains the long-term keys of both parties and uses that to recover previously agreed session keys.

parts.text: How could a web server ensure that all TLS connections it establishes with clients provide forward secrecy?
parts.solution: The web server must only allow ciphersuites which use ephemeral Diffie-Hellman for the handshake protocol.

parts.text: Why is there a fundamental problem in providing forward secrecy for electronic mail (email)?
parts.solution: Since there is no interaction it must be possible for the mail recipient to decrypt the message with the long-term key alone. Therefore forward secrecy (strictly speaking) can never be achieved for email.
